title: MAEMOD Research Workstation User Guide
text:
Welcome to the MAEMOD Research Workstation User Guide.
This guide explains how MAEMOD/MORU team members can connect to and use the shared Linux workstation for research computing tasks.
The workstation is intended for:

title: MAEMOD Research Workstation User Guide
text:
1 Workstation summary



Item
Value




Hostname
mod-linux-ws01


Operating system
Ubuntu Linux


Scheduler
Slurm


CPUs
24


Shared software location
/data/software


Shared conda environments
/data/envs

title: MAEMOD Research Workstation User Guide
text:
2 How to use this guide
New users should start with:

Quick Start
Connecting to the Workstation
Basic Linux Commands
R Package Management
Using Slurm

Users who already know Linux can go directly to the R, Python, CmdStan, and Slurm sections.

title: MAEMOD Research Workstation User Guide
text:
3 Important rule
For long or computationally intensive work, please use Slurm instead of running jobs directly in the login terminal.

title: User Accounts and Passwords
text: Each user should use their own workstation account.

title: User Accounts and Passwords
text:
1 Check your username
whoami

title: User Accounts and Passwords
text:
2 Check your groups
groups
Group membership controls access to shared folders and software.
For this workstation, users may need to be part of the research group to access shared research directories.

title: User Accounts and Passwords
text:
3 Change your password
passwd

title: User Accounts and Passwords
text:
4 Home directory
Each user has a home directory:
echo $HOME
Usually this is:
/home/username
Use your home directory for personal scripts, configuration files, and small working files.

title: User Accounts and Passwords
text:
5 Do not share passwords
Do not share your workstation password with other users.
If someone needs access, ask the administrator to create a separate account.

title: Connecting to the Workstation
text: The workstation is accessed using SSH.

title: Connecting to the Workstation
text:
1 Connection details



Item
Value




Hostname
mod-linux-ws01


Protocol
SSH


Port
22

title: Connecting to the Workstation
text:
2 Connect from Linux or macOS
Open a terminal and run:
ssh username@mod-linux-ws01
Replace username with your own username.

title: Connecting to the Workstation
section: 3 Connect from Windows Terminal or PowerShell
text:
3 Connect from Windows Terminal or PowerShell
Recent versions of Windows include an SSH client.
ssh username@mod-linux-ws01

title: Connecting to the Workstation
text:
4 Connect using PuTTY
Open PuTTY and enter:



Field
Value




Host Name
mod-linux-ws01


Port
22


Connection type
SSH



Click Open and log in with your username and password.

title: Connecting to the Workstation
text:
5 Connect using MobaXterm
MobaXterm is useful because it provides SSH, file browsing, and terminal access in one program.
Create a new SSH session:



Field
Value




Remote host
mod-linux-ws01


Username
your workstation username


Port
22

title: Connecting to the Workstation
section: 6 Connect using VS Code Remote SSH
text:
6 Connect using VS Code Remote SSH
VS Code Remote SSH is useful for editing scripts directly on the workstation.
Example SSH config entry:
Host maemod-workstation
    HostName mod-linux-ws01
    User username
Then connect to maemod-workstation from VS Code.

title: Connecting to the Workstation
text:
7 Disconnect
To log out:
exit

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
This workstation supports scientific computing with R, Python, and CmdStan.
Software is mainly managed using Miniforge, Conda, and Mamba.

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
1 Main idea
The workstation separates software into different levels:








Level
Example
Who manages it?




System software
Slurm, Quarto, LaTeX, system libraries
Administrator


Shared conda environments
R-4.4, py311
Administrator/RSE team


Project R packages
renv project library
Project users


Personal packages
user home directory
Individual user



For R projects, the recommended approach is:
shared conda R version + project-specific renv library
This means everyone may use the same R version from conda, but each serious project can keep its own R package versions using renv.

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
2 Check conda
which conda
If conda is available, this should show a path.

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
3 List available environments
conda env list
or:
mamba env list

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
4 Activate an environment
Example for R:
conda activate R-4.4
Example for Python:
conda activate py311
The exact environment names depend on what has been installed.

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
5 Deactivate an environment
conda deactivate

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
6 R
Activate the R environment:
conda activate R-4.4
Start R:
R
Run an R script:
Rscript analysis.R
Check the R package library paths:
.libPaths()

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
section: 7 Where should R packages be installed?
text:
7 Where should R packages be installed?
For most users:

quick personal tests can use the personal R library
reproducible project work should use renv
shared packages for all users should be installed by the administrator in the shared conda environment

Do not assume that installing an R package in your own account makes it available to other users.
See R Package Management for the full policy.

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
8 Python
Activate a Python environment:
conda activate py311
Start Python:
python
Run a Python script:
python analysis.py
Install Python packages using conda or mamba when possible:
mamba install numpy pandas scipy matplotlib
For project-specific work, consider creating a project-specific environment.

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
9 CmdStan
CmdStan is useful for users who prefer CmdStan or CmdStanR rather than RStan.
Check whether CmdStan is available:
ls /data/software
If using CmdStanR in R:
library(cmdstanr)
cmdstanr::cmdstan_path()
Set CmdStan path if needed:
cmdstanr::set_cmdstan_path("/data/software/cmdstan")

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
section: 10 Recommended workflow for R + CmdStanR projects
text:
10 Recommended workflow for R + CmdStanR projects
cd /data/projects/my-stan-project
conda activate R-4.4
R
Inside R:
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
install.packages("cmdstanr")
cmdstanr::set_cmdstan_path("/data/software/cmdstan")
renv::snapshot()
For future work in the same project:
cd /data/projects/my-stan-project
conda activate R-4.4
R
Then inside R:
renv::restore()

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
11 Recommended workflow
For reproducible projects:

keep scripts in a project folder
activate the correct conda environment
use renv for R package versions
run long jobs through Slurm
save outputs to results/
use Git when possible

title: Using R, Python, and CmdStan
text:
12 Example environment note
Create a file called environment-notes.txt in your project folder:
Date: 2026-06-18
Workstation: mod-linux-ws01
Environment: R-4.4
R package management: renv
Main packages: tidyverse, cmdstanr, posterior

title: Quick Start
text: This page gives the shortest path to start using the workstation.

title: Quick Start
text:
1 1. Connect to the workstation
Replace username with your own username.
ssh username@mod-linux-ws01
If you are using Windows, you can connect using PuTTY, MobaXterm, Windows Terminal, or VS Code Remote SSH.

title: Quick Start
text:
2 2. Check where you are
hostname
whoami
pwd

title: Quick Start
text:
3 3. Check the workstation status
sinfo
This shows whether the Slurm compute partition is available.

title: Quick Start
text:
4 4. Check your files
ls
ls -lh

title: Quick Start
text:
5 5. Activate a software environment
Example for R:
conda activate R-4.4
Example for Python:
conda activate py311
The exact environment names may change. To see available conda environments:
conda env list
or:
mamba env list

title: Quick Start
text:
6 6. Submit a job
Create a file called job.sh:
nano job.sh
Example Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=test-job
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=1
#SBATCH --mem=2G
#SBATCH --output=test-job.out

hostname
date
Submit it:
sbatch job.sh
Check the queue:
squeue
Check the output:
cat test-job.out

title: Quick Start
text:
7 7. Log out
exit

title: Getting Started
text: This page introduces the basic information needed before using the MAEMOD research workstation.

title: Getting Started
text:
1 What you need
Before logging in, you need:

a workstation user account
a password
network access to the workstation
an SSH client

If you do not yet have an account, contact the workstation administrator.

title: Getting Started
text:
2 First login
From Linux, macOS, or Windows Terminal:
ssh username@mod-linux-ws01
Replace username with your own workstation username.

title: Getting Started
text:
3 Change your password
After your first login, change your password:
passwd
Follow the instructions on screen.

title: Getting Started
text:
4 Check your username
whoami

title: Getting Started
text:
5 Check the hostname
hostname
You should see:
mod-linux-ws01

title: Getting Started
text:
6 Check your home directory
pwd
Usually this will show something like:
/home/username

title: Getting Started
text:
7 Check system status
sinfo
This checks the Slurm scheduler.

title: Getting Started
text:
8 Basic etiquette
Please avoid running heavy computations directly in the login shell.
For long analyses, simulations, model fitting, or parallel jobs, use Slurm.

title: Submit an R Job
text: This example shows how to submit an R script using Slurm.

title: Submit an R Job
text:
1 Example R script
Create analysis.R:
print("Running R analysis")
print(Sys.info())
print(Sys.time())

title: Submit an R Job
text:
2 Example Slurm script
Create run-r.sh:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=r-test
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=1
#SBATCH --mem=2G
#SBATCH --output=r-test.out
#SBATCH --error=r-test.err

conda activate R-4.4

Rscript analysis.R

title: Submit an R Job
text:
3 Submit the job
sbatch run-r.sh

title: Submit an R Job
text:
4 Check the queue
squeue -u $USER

title: Submit an R Job
text:
5 Check output
cat r-test.out

title: Submit an R Job
text:
6 Notes
If conda activate does not work inside the Slurm script, you may need to initialise conda first or use the full path to the R executable inside the environment.

title: Submit a CmdStan Job
text: This example shows how to run a CmdStan model using Slurm.

title: Submit a CmdStan Job
Submit a CmdStan Job
text:
2 Compile the model
If CmdStan is available in your environment:
make normal
or use CmdStanR from R.

title: Submit a CmdStan Job
text:
3 Example Slurm script for CmdStan
Create run-cmdstan.sh:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=cmdstan-test
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
#SBATCH --mem=8G
#SBATCH --output=cmdstan-test.out
#SBATCH --error=cmdstan-test.err

export STAN_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK

./normal sample data file=data.json output file=output.csv

title: Submit a CmdStan Job
text:
4 Submit the job
sbatch run-cmdstan.sh

title: Submit a CmdStan Job
text:
5 Check output
cat cmdstan-test.out

title: Submit a CmdStan Job
text:
6 Notes
CmdStan models can use multiple CPU threads when the model and settings support threading.
Only request multiple CPUs if your model can use them.

title: Admin Notes
text: This section is intended for workstation administrators.

title: Admin Notes
text:
1 User management
Create a new user:
sudo adduser username
Add user to a group:
sudo usermod -aG research username
Check user groups:
groups username

title: Admin Notes
text:
2 Shared directories
Shared software:
/data/software
Shared conda environments:
/data/envs
Check permissions:
ls -ld /data/software
ls -ld /data/envs

title: Admin Notes
text:
3 Slurm checks
Check node status:
sinfo
Show node details:
scontrol show node mod-linux-ws01
Restart Slurm services if needed:
sudo systemctl restart slurmctld
sudo systemctl restart slurmd
Check service status:
systemctl status slurmctld
systemctl status slurmd

title: Admin Notes
text:
4 Quarto
Check Quarto:
quarto --version
quarto check

title: Admin Notes
text:
5 Conda and Mamba
Check installation:
which conda
which mamba
List environments:
conda env list

title: Admin Notes
text:
6 Documentation website
Render the guide:
quarto render
Preview locally:
quarto preview
The rendered website is written to:
_site/

title: Admin Notes
text:
7 Notes for future administrators
Keep this section updated when the workstation configuration changes.

title: Basic Linux Commands
text: This page lists basic Linux commands that are useful for new users.

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
1 Where am I?
Show the current directory:
pwd

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
2 List files
ls
Show details:
ls -lh
Show hidden files:
ls -la

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
3 Change directory
Go to a folder:
cd folder_name
Go back to your home directory:
cd
Go up one level:
cd ..

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
4 Create folders
mkdir project
Create nested folders:
mkdir -p project/data/raw

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
5 Copy files
Copy one file:
cp file.txt backup.txt
Copy a folder:
cp -r project project_backup

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
6 Move or rename files
Rename a file:
mv old_name.txt new_name.txt
Move a file into a folder:
mv file.txt project/

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
7 Delete files
Delete a file:
rm file.txt
Delete a folder and everything inside it:
rm -r folder_name






Warning



Be careful with rm. Files deleted from the command line may not be recoverable.

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
8 View text files
Show a whole file:
cat file.txt
View a long file page by page:
less file.txt
Show the first lines:
head file.txt
Show the last lines:
tail file.txt
Watch a file as it updates:
tail -f output.log

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
9 Edit text files
Use nano for simple editing:
nano script.R
Save with Ctrl + O, then press Enter. Exit with Ctrl + X.

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
10 Search inside files
Search for text in a file:
grep "error" output.log
Search recursively in a folder:
grep -r "parameter" .

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
11 Find files
Find files by name:
find . -name "*.R"
Find large files:
find . -type f -size +1G

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
12 Check disk space
df -h
Check the size of a folder:
du -sh folder_name

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
13 Check memory
free -h

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
14 Check running processes
top
or, if installed:
htop

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
15 Stop a running command
Press:
Ctrl + C

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
16 Command history
Show previous commands:
history
Search command history:
Ctrl + R

title: Basic Linux Commands
text:
17 Useful shortcuts



Shortcut
Meaning




Tab
autocomplete file or command name


Ctrl + C
stop current command


Ctrl + L
clear screen


Ctrl + A
move to start of line


Ctrl + E
move to end of line

title: Submit a Python Job
text: This example shows how to submit a Python script using Slurm.

title: Submit a Python Job
text:
1 Example Python script
Create analysis.py:
import platform
from datetime import datetime

print("Running Python analysis")
print(platform.node())
print(datetime.now())

title: Submit a Python Job
text:
2 Example Slurm script
Create run-python.sh:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=python-test
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=1
#SBATCH --mem=2G
#SBATCH --output=python-test.out
#SBATCH --error=python-test.err

conda activate py311

python analysis.py

title: Submit a Python Job
text:
3 Submit the job
sbatch run-python.sh

title: Submit a Python Job
text:
4 Check output
cat python-test.out

title: Submit a Python Job
text:
5 Notes
For larger analyses, request more memory or CPUs only when needed.
Example:
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
#SBATCH --mem=8G

title: File Storage and Project Folders
text: This page explains where files should be stored.

title: File Storage and Project Folders
text:
1 Home directory
Your home directory is:
/home/username
Use this for:

personal scripts
small analyses
configuration files
notes

Avoid storing very large datasets in your home directory.

title: File Storage and Project Folders
text:
2 Shared software
Shared software is installed in:
/data/software
This directory is managed by the workstation administrator.
Users should not normally install project data here.

title: File Storage and Project Folders
text:
3 Shared conda environments
Shared conda environments are stored in:
/data/envs
These environments can be used by multiple users.
Example:
conda activate /data/envs/R-4.4
or, if the environment name is configured:
conda activate R-4.4

title: File Storage and Project Folders
text:
4 Project folders
Project data should be stored in a shared project location, for example:
/data/projects
A suggested project structure is:
project-name/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data/
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ raw/
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ processed/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ scripts/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ results/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ figures/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ reports/
в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md

title: File Storage and Project Folders
text:
5 Recommended practice
Keep raw data separate from processed data.
Do not overwrite raw data.
Use clear filenames and include dates or version numbers where useful.
Example:
results/model-fit-2026-06-18.csv

title: File Storage and Project Folders
text:
6 Check disk usage
Check available disk space:
df -h
Check the size of your folder:
du -sh .

title: Good Practice
text: This page gives recommended practices for using the shared workstation.

title: Good Practice
text:
1 Use Slurm for long jobs
Do not run long or intensive jobs directly in the login terminal.
Use Slurm:
sbatch job.sh

title: Good Practice
text:
2 Organise project folders
Recommended structure:
project-name/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ scripts/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ results/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ figures/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ reports/
в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md

title: Good Practice
text:
3 Keep raw data unchanged
Do not edit raw data directly.
Save cleaned or transformed data in a separate folder.

title: Good Practice
text:
4 Use clear filenames
Good examples:
analysis-model-v1.R
results-2026-06-18.csv
figure-transmission-map.png
Avoid:
new.R
test2.csv
final_final_v3.csv

title: Good Practice
text:
5 Record software versions
Useful commands:
R --version
python --version
quarto --version
For R:
sessionInfo()
For Python:
pip freeze
or:
conda list

title: Good Practice
text:
6 Use Git for code
Use Git for scripts, reports, and documentation.
Do not put very large data files into Git unless there is a clear reason.

title: Good Practice
text:
7 Clean up old files
Check folder size:
du -sh .
Remove temporary files that are no longer needed.

title: Good Practice
text:
8 Be considerate
This is a shared workstation.
Please avoid using all CPUs or memory unless necessary.

title: R Package Management
text:
This page explains how R packages should be installed and managed on the shared workstation.
The workstation uses shared R installations through conda environments, for example:
The recommended approach is:

title: R Package Management
text:
1 Why package management matters
Different projects may need different versions of R packages.
For example, one project may require an older version of brms, while another project may need the newest version of tidyverse.
If everyone installs packages into the same shared R library, one userвЂ™s package update may break another userвЂ™s project.

title: R Package Management
text:
2 Recommended policy







Scenario
Recommended approach




Quick test or small script
Use the shared R conda environment


Personal analysis
Use your personal R library or renv


Project with collaborators
Use renv


Manuscript or reproducible analysis
Use renv


Package needs Linux system libraries
Ask the administrator


Software should be available to all users
Ask the administrator to install it in the shared conda environment

title: R Package Management
section: 3 Check which R you are using
text:
3 Check which R you are using
After activating an environment:
conda activate R-4.4
which R
R --version
Inside R:
R.home()
.libPaths()
.libPaths() shows where R will look for packages.

title: R Package Management
text:
4 Personal R package library
If you install packages without administrator permission, R may install them into your personal library.
This is usually inside your home directory, for example:
/home/username/R/x86_64-conda-linux-gnu-library/4.4
This is acceptable for personal work, but it is not ideal for shared projects because other users may not have the same packages.

title: R Package Management
section: 5 Installing R packages for personal use
text:
5 Installing R packages for personal use
Activate the R environment:
conda activate R-4.4
R
Then in R:
install.packages("tidyverse")
Check where the package was installed:
find.package("tidyverse")

title: R Package Management
text:
6 Using renv for a project
renv creates a project-specific R package library and records package versions in a lockfile.
This is the recommended approach for collaborative and reproducible projects.

6.1 Step 1: Go to your project folder
cd /data/projects/my-project
conda activate R-4.4
R


6.2 Step 2: Initialise renv
Inside R:
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
This creates project files such as:
renv/
renv.lock
.Rprofile


6.3 Step 3: Install packages inside the project
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("cmdstanr")


6.4 Step 4: Save the package versions
renv::snapshot()
This updates:
renv.lock
The renv.lock file should be committed to Git.


6.5 Step 5: Restore the project on another account or machine
When another user opens the same project, they can run:
renv::restore()
This installs the package versions recorded in renv.lock.

title: R Package Management
section: 7 What should be committed to Git?
text:
7 What should be committed to Git?
Commit these files:
renv.lock
.Rprofile
renv/settings.json
Do not commit the full package library:
renv/library/
The package library can be recreated using:
renv::restore()

title: R Package Management
text:
8 Recommended project structure with renv
project-name/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ scripts/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ results/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ renv/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ renv.lock
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ .Rprofile
в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md

title: R Package Management
text:
9 When not to use renv
You may not need renv for:

very small one-off scripts
teaching examples
quick checks
temporary exploratory work

For anything that may become a paper, report, Shiny app, or shared analysis, use renv.

title: R Package Management
section: 10 Installing packages into the shared conda R environment
text:
10 Installing packages into the shared conda R environment
Only administrators should install packages into the shared R conda environment.
Example administrator workflow:
conda activate R-4.4
mamba install -c conda-forge r-tidyverse r-data.table r-cmdstanr
This is useful for common packages needed by many users.
However, not every R package needs to be installed globally.

title: R Package Management
section: 11 R packages from CRAN vs conda-forge
text:
11 R packages from CRAN vs conda-forge
There are two common ways to install R packages:

11.1 From inside R
install.packages("package_name")


11.2 From conda-forge
mamba install -c conda-forge r-package_name
For shared environments, conda-forge packages are often easier to manage at the system level.
For project-specific work, renv is usually better.

title: R Package Management
text:
12 Packages requiring Linux system libraries
Some R packages need Linux system libraries before they can be installed.
Examples include packages that depend on:

XML
SSL
curl
GDAL
GEOS
PROJ
Java

If installation fails with missing system libraries, ask the administrator.
Do not repeatedly try random fixes in the shared environment.

title: R Package Management
text:
13 Snap packages
Snap packages are mainly useful for standalone applications, especially desktop apps or command-line tools packaged as snaps.
Snap is not recommended for managing R packages.
Use this rule:







Need
Use




R package
renv, CRAN, or conda-forge


Python package
conda, mamba, pip, or project environment


System package
apt, administrator-managed


Standalone application packaged as snap
snap, administrator-managed

title: R Package Management
text:
14 Check installed snap packages
snap list

title: R Package Management
text:
15 Install a snap package
Only administrators should install snap packages system-wide.
Example:
sudo snap install package-name
Some snap packages require classic confinement:
sudo snap install package-name --classic

title: R Package Management
text:
16 Summary
For most R users:
conda activate R-4.4
cd /data/projects/my-project
R
Then inside R:
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
install.packages("tidyverse")
renv::snapshot()
For collaborators:
renv::restore()

title: Using Slurm
text:
Slurm is used to manage jobs on the workstation.
Please use Slurm for long-running or computationally intensive work.

title: Using Slurm
text:
1 Check node status
sinfo
Example output may show:
PARTITION AVAIL  TIMELIMIT  NODES  STATE NODELIST
compute*     up   infinite      1   idle mod-linux-ws01

title: Using Slurm
text:
2 Check running jobs
squeue
Show only your jobs:
squeue -u $USER

title: Using Slurm
text:
3 Submit a job
Create a job script, for example job.sh:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=my-job
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=1
#SBATCH --mem=2G
#SBATCH --output=my-job.out

hostname
date
Submit the job:
sbatch job.sh

title: Using Slurm
text:
4 Cancel a job
scancel JOBID
Replace JOBID with the job ID shown by squeue.

title: Using Slurm
text:
5 Interactive job
Use this when you need an interactive shell with allocated resources:
srun --cpus-per-task=4 --mem=8G --pty bash

title: Using Slurm
text:
6 Request CPUs
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
Use more CPUs only if your program can actually use them.

title: Using Slurm
text:
7 Request memory
#SBATCH --mem=8G
If a job fails because of memory, increase this value.

title: Using Slurm
text:
8 Output files
This line writes job output to a file:
#SBATCH --output=my-job.out
For error output:
#SBATCH --error=my-job.err

title: Using Slurm
text:
9 Check job details
scontrol show job JOBID

title: Using Slurm
text:
10 Good Slurm habits

do not request all CPUs unless necessary
do not request excessive memory
save output files clearly
check logs after jobs finish
cancel jobs that are no longer needed

title: Troubleshooting
text: This page lists common problems and possible solutions.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
1 Cannot connect by SSH
Check:

Are you connected to the correct network?
Is the hostname correct?
Is your username correct?
Is the workstation online?

Try:
ssh username@mod-linux-ws01

title: Troubleshooting
text:
2 Permission denied
Check your username:
whoami
Check your groups:
groups
You may need to be added to the correct group.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
3 Conda command not found
Check:
which conda
If nothing appears, conda may not be loaded in your shell.
Try logging out and logging in again.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
4 Environment not found
List environments:
conda env list
Check the exact environment name.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
5 Job is pending
Check queue:
squeue
Check job details:
scontrol show job JOBID
Common reasons:

not enough requested resources available
Slurm configuration issue
node is busy

title: Troubleshooting
section: 6 Job ran but produced no output
text:
6 Job ran but produced no output
Check your Slurm output file:
ls -lh
cat job-name.out
cat job-name.err
Check whether your script actually writes output.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
7 Out of memory
If the error suggests memory problems, increase memory request:
#SBATCH --mem=8G
Use only as much as needed.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
8 Disk full
Check disk space:
df -h
Check folder size:
du -sh .
Find large files:
find . -type f -size +1G

title: Troubleshooting
text:
9 Command not found
Check whether the software is installed:
which command_name
Example:
which R
which python
which quarto
You may need to activate the correct environment.
